title: The Little Laws of Life
topic: Human behavior, timeless patterns, and decision-making
learningGoals: 能理解 what changes 與 what never changes 的對比架構。
能辨認作者如何用例子引出抽象主題：人性、行為、長期判斷。
能學會心理、商業、歷史相關單字，並應用在摘要與推論題。
能練習 rhetorical question、contrast、relative clause 與 abstract noun 的閱讀判斷。

paragraphs.english: A lunch conversation about Warren Buffett and Snickers introduces the lesson. Even after a crisis crippled the global economy, one ordinary candy bar stayed bestselling. The example feels small, but it points to something timeless.
paragraphs.chinese: 一場關於華倫・巴菲特與 Snickers 巧克力棒的午餐談話，引出了這個單元。即使一場危機重創全球經濟，一個普通的巧克力棒仍然維持暢銷。這個例子看似很小，卻指向某種不變的事物。

paragraphs.english: Technology, medicine, geopolitics, and dialects can change quickly. Yet greed, jealousy, overconfidence, shortsightedness, and the desire for certainty keep appearing in human behavior. That pattern is relatable because it is universal.
paragraphs.chinese: 科技、醫學、地緣政治與語言方言都可能快速改變。然而，貪婪、嫉妒、過度自信、短視，以及對確定性的渴望，仍然不斷出現在人類行為中。這個模式讓人有共鳴，因為它具有普遍性。

paragraphs.english: The lesson asks readers not only to predict what will change, but also to grasp what will remain the same. Stable truths are easy to overlook because change captures attention. A good thinker studies both movement and stillness.
paragraphs.chinese: 這個單元提醒讀者，不只要預測什麼會改變，也要理解什麼會保持不變。穩定的真理很容易被忽略，因為變化更能吸引注意力。好的思考者會同時研究變動與不變。

paragraphs.english: The chapter works like a preview of little laws about life. These laws do not promise certainty, but they help us contemplate risk, attachment, luck, and behavior. When a pattern appears in parallel across time, it may deserve our attention.
paragraphs.chinese: 這一章像是人生小法則的預告。這些法則不保證確定性，但能幫助我們思考風險、依戀、運氣與行為。當某個模式在不同時代平行出現時，它可能值得我們注意。

vocabulary.englishDefinition: damaged something so badly that it could not work normally
vocabulary.sourceSentence: A crisis crippled the global economy.
One ordinary candy bar stayed bestselling.

vocabulary.chineseDefinition: 不受時間影響的；永恆的
vocabulary.englishDefinition: not changing or becoming less important over time
vocabulary.example: Human behavior may reveal timeless patterns.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: The example points to something timeless.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: how geography and politics affect countries and power
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Technology, medicine, geopolitics, and dialects can change quickly.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: a form of a language used in a particular area or by a particular group
vocabulary.example: Dialects may shift as communities change.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: a strong desire for more money, power, or things than needed
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Greed keeps appearing in human behavior.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: an unhappy feeling caused by wanting what someone else has
vocabulary.example: Jealousy is one pattern that does not disappear.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Jealousy keeps appearing in human behavior.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: too much confidence in one's own ability or judgment
vocabulary.example: Overconfidence can make people ignore risk.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Overconfidence keeps appearing in human behavior.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: failure to think about future results
vocabulary.example: Shortsightedness can lead to poor choices.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Shortsightedness keeps appearing in human behavior.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: the state of being completely sure
vocabulary.example: People often want certainty in an uncertain world.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: The desire for certainty keeps appearing in human behavior.

vocabulary.englishDefinition: easy to understand because it feels connected to one's own experience
vocabulary.example: The pattern is relatable because everyone has seen it.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: That pattern is relatable because it is universal.

vocabulary.example: A universal pattern can appear across many cultures.
Readers should grasp what remains the same.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Readers should grasp what will remain the same.

vocabulary.example: Stable truths are easy to overlook.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: Stable truths are easy to overlook.

vocabulary.example: The chapter helps readers contemplate risk.
vocabulary.sourceSentence: The laws help us contemplate risk, attachment, luck, and behavior.

phrases.example: A company may bounce back after a difficult year.
Human behavior can be same as ever even when tools change.
The lesson focuses on what never changes.
Change captures attention more easily than stability.
Timeless patterns help us make sense of the future.

grammar.explanation: A question can introduce a thinking frame instead of asking for a simple answer.
grammar.example: What will change, and what will remain the same?

grammar.explanation: Words such as even after, yet, but, and not only help compare changing details with stable patterns.
grammar.example: Technology changes quickly, yet human behavior often repeats.

grammar.explanation: What + verb can act like a noun phrase in abstract discussion.
grammar.example: Readers should grasp what will remain the same.

importantSentences.sentence: Change captures attention, but stable behavior teaches prediction.
importantSentences.note: 對比句：change 與 stable behavior 是全文核心。

importantSentences.sentence: The better question is not only what will change, but what will not change.
importantSentences.note: 文法重點：not only...but... 與 what-clause。

importantSentences.sentence: When a pattern appears in parallel across time, it may deserve our attention.
importantSentences.note: 推論句：跨時代重複出現的模式，就是作者要讀者重視的線索。

practice.question: What is the main idea of the lesson?
options: Candy bars are the most important part of history.
Some human patterns remain stable even as the world changes.
practice.answer: Some human patterns remain stable even as the world changes.

practice.question: Why does the lesson mention Snickers?
options: To show a small example of lasting consumer behavior.
To compare different kinds of chocolate.
To explain how candy is made.
To argue that all companies are the same.
practice.answer: To show a small example of lasting consumer behavior.

practice.question: Which pair best shows the lesson's main contrast?
The word overlook is closest in meaning to ________.
What can be inferred about stable truths?
options: They may be less exciting than change, but they are useful for judgment.
They are easy to predict perfectly.
practice.answer: They may be less exciting than change, but they are useful for judgment.

practice.question: The author's attitude is best described as ________.

answerKey.question: What is the main idea of the lesson?
answerKey.answer: Some human patterns remain stable even as the world changes.

answerKey.answer: To show a small example of lasting consumer behavior.

answerKey.question: Which pair best shows the lesson's main contrast?
The word overlook is closest in meaning to ________.
What can be inferred about stable truths?
answerKey.answer: They may be less exciting than change, but they are useful for judgment.

answerKey.question: The author's attitude is best described as ________.

source.type: user-provided HTML excerpt transformed into a public lesson
source.title: Same as Ever: A Guide to What Never Changes
source.author: Morgan Housel
source.note: This public interactive lesson uses transformed summaries, vocabulary, short teaching sentences, and practice. It does not reproduce the full user-provided chapter text.
